cards.title: Setting Up a BNYS Clinic — Registration & Licensing

alerts.label: Legal Framework for BNYS Practice in India
alerts.text: BNYS practitioners are registered under the Central Council of Indian Medicine (CCIM) Act, 1970. Each state has its own State Medical Council for AYUSH practitioners. Registration is mandatory before practising. Understanding the regulatory framework protects you legally and defines your scope of practice clearly.

rows: Register with your State AYUSH Medical Council after graduation. Required for legal practice. Application: degree certificate, marksheets, internship completion certificate, photos, registration fee.
State AYUSH Medical Council (e.g., Karnataka AYUSH Medical Council, Tamil Nadu Board of Indian Medicine)
Register clinic with local municipal authority / panchayat / district health office. Required for running an inpatient facility or charging fees. Obtain trade licence from municipality.

Clinical Establishments (Registration and Regulation) Act, 2010 applies to clinics in states that have adopted it (most states). Register within 90 days of opening. Fee varies by state. Renewal annual.
Hospitals and clinics generating bio-medical waste must register with State Pollution Control Board. Even small clinics using needles or dressings must comply with BMW Management Rules, 2016.

If dispensing AYUSH medicines: separate drug licence under Drugs and Cosmetics Act. Ayurvedic/naturopathic formulations may be dispensed under Schedule A of the Act without pharmacist in some states — check your state rules.

If annual turnover exceeds ₹20 lakh (₹10 lakh for special category states): GST registration mandatory. Healthcare services are generally exempt from GST, but pharmacy sales, cosmetic procedures, and wellness packages may attract GST.

cards._legacyHtml:
Legal Framework for BNYS Practice in India
BNYS practitioners are registered under the Central Council of Indian Medicine (CCIM) Act, 1970. Each state has its own State Medical Council for AYUSH practitioners. Registration is mandatory before practising. Understanding the regulatory framework protects you legally and defines your scope of practice clearly.
RequirementDetailsAuthority
BNYS Degree RegistrationRegister with your State AYUSH Medical Council after graduation. Required for legal practice. Application: degree certificate, marksheets, internship completion certificate, photos, registration fee.State AYUSH Medical Council (e.g., Karnataka AYUSH Medical Council, Tamil Nadu Board of Indian Medicine)
Clinic RegistrationRegister clinic with local municipal authority / panchayat / district health office. Required for running an inpatient facility or charging fees. Obtain trade licence from municipality.District Health Officer / Municipal Corporation
Clinical Establishment ActClinical Establishments (Registration and Regulation) Act, 2010 applies to clinics in states that have adopted it (most states). Register within 90 days of opening. Fee varies by state. Renewal annual.District Appropriate Authority (usually CMO level)
Bio-Medical WasteHospitals and clinics generating bio-medical waste must register with State Pollution Control Board. Even small clinics using needles or dressings must comply with BMW Management Rules, 2016.State Pollution Control Board
Pharmacy / DispensaryIf dispensing AYUSH medicines: separate drug licence under Drugs and Cosmetics Act. Ayurvedic/naturopathic formulations may be dispensed under Schedule A of the Act without pharmacist in some states — check your state rules.State Drug Controller
GST RegistrationIf annual turnover exceeds ₹20 lakh (₹10 lakh for special category states): GST registration mandatory. Healthcare services are generally exempt from GST, but pharmacy sales, cosmetic procedures, and wellness packages may attract GST.GST Department

cards.title: Clinic Setup — Infrastructure & Equipment

steps.title: Minimum Space Requirements
steps.text: Consultation room: minimum 10×10 ft. Examination area with curtain partition. Hydrotherapy facility: separate room with good drainage, non-slip flooring, hot and cold water supply, multiple treatment baths (hip bath, foot bath, spinal bath tubs). Mud therapy room: separate (for cleanliness). Yoga hall: minimum 20×20 ft for group classes. Reception/waiting: proportional to patient volume. Toilet facilities: separate for patients and staff. All areas: natural light + ventilation (naturopathic principle).

steps.title: Essential Equipment Checklist — BNYS Clinic
steps.text: Examination: examination table, stethoscope, BP apparatus (manual + digital), pulse oximeter, thermometer, tongue depressors, reflex hammer, ophthalmoscope, otoscope, measuring tape, weighing scale with height measure. Hydrotherapy: hip bath tubs (hot/cold/neutral), foot bath tubs, full bath tub, steam cabinet or steam generator, hot and cold water supply with mixing valve, thermometer for water. Mud therapy: mud tray, cotton muslin cloth, mixing vessels. Yoga: mats, bolsters, blocks, straps, chairs. Physiotherapy (if applicable): TENS unit, heating pads, cold packs.

steps.title: Digital Infrastructure
steps.text: Electronic Health Records (EHR): cloud-based patient management software — recommended: Practo, Clinic365, or free option: GNU Health. Online appointment booking: Google Business Profile + PhonePe/Razorpay for payments. WhatsApp Business: patient communication, appointment reminders, report sharing. Telemedicine: Practo, DocsApp, or direct video consult compliance (MCI telemedicine guidelines 2020 — BNYS practitioners can practise telemedicine). Patient education: create condition-specific handouts (use AI tools in this app!).

cards.title: Patient Retention & Practice Growth

rows: 45–60 min initial consultation (never less). Thorough assessment — patients feel heard. Printed treatment plan with clear goals. Follow-up appointment booked before patient leaves.
80% return rate if initial consultation is thorough and rapport is established

Use validated tools at every visit: PHQ-9 (depression), NRS (pain 0-10), blood glucose log, BP diary. Show patients their progress graphically. "Your pain score was 8/10 six weeks ago, today it is 4/10."
Demonstrates value, builds trust, creates data for testimonials and case studies
Check-in message 3–5 days after each appointment: "How are you finding the new diet changes? Any questions?" Brief, professional, timely.
Significantly ↑ compliance and retention. Patients feel cared for between appointments.
Build relationships with GPs, orthopaedic surgeons, gynaecologists, diabetologists in your area. Write professional referral notes. Refer to them → they refer back. Attend CME events.
Sustainable referral pipeline within 6–12 months. Bidirectional referral is the best practice-builder.

6–8 week structured programmes: "Diabetes Reversal Programme," "Weight Management," "Stress & Sleep." Group format: community, accountability, higher perceived value, better patient outcomes, higher revenue per hour.
Premium pricing justified, high completion rates, strong testimonials
One educational post per week on Instagram/Facebook: tip, myth-bust, patient education (anonymised case). Use Canva. Consistency > frequency.
Brand authority, patient trust, local SEO — patients find you when searching for BNYS + your area

pearls.label: 🟡 Avoid These Common Mistakes
pearls.text: 1. Undercharging — patients associate price with quality. 2. Seeing too many patients per day (burnout and poor quality care). 3. No written consent for treatments — medico-legal risk. 4. Not documenting case notes — "if it isn't written, it didn't happen." 5. Competing with allopathy instead of differentiating — your value is TIME, holistic care, non-pharmacological solutions. 6. Neglecting your own health — practitioners who model lifestyle medicine are more credible and more effective. 7. No referral system — knowing your limits and referring appropriately builds, not diminishes, your reputation.

Group format: community, accountability, higher perceived value, better patient outcomes, higher revenue per hour.Premium pricing justified, high completion rates, strong testimonials

🟡 Avoid These Common Mistakes
1. Undercharging — patients associate price with quality. 2. Seeing too many patients per day (burnout and poor quality care). 3. No written consent for treatments — medico-legal risk. 4. Not documenting case notes — "if it isn't written, it didn't happen." 5. Competing with allopathy instead of differentiating — your value is TIME, holistic care, non-pharmacological solutions. 6. Neglecting your own health — practitioners who model lifestyle medicine are more credible and more effective. 7. No referral system — knowing your limits and referring appropriately builds, not diminishes, your reputation.

cards.title: Medico-Legal Essentials & Informed Consent

alerts.label: Medico-Legal Protection — Every BNYS Practitioner Needs This
alerts.text: Legal claims against complementary medicine practitioners are increasing in India. The best protection is: comprehensive documentation, valid informed consent, clear scope-of-practice awareness, and timely referral. These four pillars are both ethically correct and legally protective.

steps.title: Informed Consent — What Must Be Documented
steps.text: Every treatment requires: (1) Name of treatment/procedure. (2) Nature and purpose of treatment. (3) Expected benefits. (4) Material risks and side effects. (5) Alternatives available. (6) Consequences of declining treatment. (7) Patient's right to withdraw consent at any time. For invasive procedures (leech therapy, acupuncture, manipulation): written consent mandatory. Keep signed consent forms minimum 7 years. Verbal consent: document in notes with date and what was explained.

steps.title: Documentation Standards
steps.text: Every patient contact must be documented in writing. SOAP format minimum: Subjective (history), Objective (examination), Assessment (diagnosis/clinical impression), Plan (treatment). Include: date, time, presenting complaint, findings, advice given, prescriptions/referrals, follow-up plan, your signature. Abbreviations: use only standard ones. Never alter records retrospectively — draw single line through errors, initial and date. Electronic records: maintain backup and audit trail.

steps.title: Scope of Practice — Know Your Limits
steps.text: BNYS practitioners: authorised to diagnose and treat using naturopathic/yogic modalities. Prescribing modern medicine (allopathic drugs): NOT within scope — refer. BNYS practitioners may NOT perform surgical procedures except those explicitly within AYUSH scope. Emergency situations: provide first aid, stabilise, and REFER immediately — never delay emergency care trying to treat naturopathically. Document every referral: to whom, for what, and the clinical reason.

Document every referral: to whom, for what, and the clinical reason.
